steps.prompt: Which words are the main verbs?

hints.Senatus, consul: 'Senatus' and 'consul' are nouns — the subjects of the two clauses
hints.haec, intellegit: 'haec' is a pronoun meaning 'these things' — only 'intellegit' is a verb
hints.Senatus, videt: 'Senatus' is a noun (the senate) — only 'videt' is a verb

hints.to read: 'to read' is 'legere' — 'intellegere' adds the prefix inter- meaning 'to understand'
hints.to choose: 'to choose' is 'eligere' — a different compound of 'legere'

hints.Perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'intellexit' — 'intellegit' with the -it present ending is present tense
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'intellegat' — 'intellegit' is indicative

hints.lego, legere, legi, lectum: These are the parts of 'legere' (to read/gather) — the base verb without the prefix
hints.eligo, eligere, elegi, electum: These are the parts of 'eligere' (to choose) — a different compound of 'legere'
hints.neglego, neglegere, neglexi, neglectum: These are the parts of 'neglegere' (to neglect) — similar pattern but different prefix

hints.to live: 'to live' is 'vivere' — similar start but different verb
hints.to seem: 'to seem' is the passive 'videri' — the active 'videre' means 'to see'

hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivere' (to live)
hints.venio, venire, veni, ventum: These are the parts of 'venire' (to come)

hints.Genitive: Genitive singular of 4th declension would also be 'senatus', but here it is the subject of 'intellegit'
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'senatui' — 'senatus' is nominative, the subject
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'senatum' — 'senatus' is nominative here

hints.2nd: 2nd declension -us nouns have gen. -i — 'senatus' has gen. 'senatus' (4th declension)
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have varied endings — 'senatus' with gen. -us is 4th declension
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'senatus' is 4th declension

hints.Genitive: Genitive would be 'consulis' — 'consul' is nominative, subject of 'videt'
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'consulem' — 'consul' is the nominative subject
hints.Vocative: Vocative would look the same, but Cicero is referring to himself in the 3rd person as consul, not addressing a consul

steps.prompt: Why does Cicero refer to himself as 'consul' in the third person?
steps.target: To emphasize the authority of his office
choices: To emphasize the authority of his office
He is speaking about a different consul

hints.He is quoting someone else: Cicero is speaking in his own voice — the third person is a deliberate rhetorical choice
hints.He is being modest: Using his title 'consul' is the opposite of modesty — it invokes official power
hints.He is speaking about a different consul: Cicero was the consul addressing the senate — he refers to himself by title for rhetorical weight

translation: The senate understands these things; the consul sees them.
